intro: The SHE Score is a data-backed 0–100 measure of how good life is for women in a country — where higher always means better. It is built from independent institutional data (UN Women, World Bank, WHO, UNODC, UNESCO and ILO) and published annually, quarterly for registered governments.

It is a composite of eight weighted pillars, with a Violence Penalty subtracted from the total. Every indicator is normalised to 0–100 and inverted where needed (so a high maternal-mortality rate, pay gap or rape rate lowers the score, never raises it).
disclaimer: The SHE Score is an independent project and is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or derived from the UNDP/UN Women Women's Empowerment Index, the SHE Index powered by EY, or any other index referenced on this site.

pillars.name: Empowerment
pillars.desc: Parliamentary seats, ministerial roles, legal rights, freedom of movement.

pillars.name: Bodily Autonomy
pillars.desc: Reproductive rights, child marriage, FGM, period poverty.

pillars.name: Safety & Justice
pillars.desc: DV laws, femicide, honour-based violence, legal aid, police responsiveness.

pillars.name: Education
pillars.desc: Literacy, enrollment, STEM, menstrual barriers to attendance.

pillars.name: Economic Inclusion
pillars.desc: Pay gap, formal employment, banking access, property rights.

pillars.name: Health & Survival
pillars.desc: Maternal mortality, life expectancy, anaemia, cancer screening.

pillars.name: Dignity & Welfare
pillars.desc: Widow rights, caregiver burden, food insecurity, mental health.

pillars.name: Digital & Social
pillars.desc: Online harassment, internet & mobile gender gaps.

pillars.name: Violence Penalty
pillars.desc: Rape, acid attacks, dowry violence, femicide — subtracted from the composite, never added.

sources: World Bank Gender Data Portal & Women, Business and the Law (WBL)
UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)
cadence: Scores are published annually, and quarterly for registered governments. Between publications, a signal layer surfaces qualitative momentum from 100+ multilingual news sources, arXiv, PubMed and GDELT — these signals are indicative context, not a change to the published score.

faq.q: Is the SHE Score the same as the UN's Women's Empowerment Index?
faq.a: No. The SHE Score is an independent index with a different methodology and publisher (SHE Foundation), and unlike the UN/UN Women WEI it is investable via $SHE. See exactly how the two relate — alongside every other major gender index — on The Landscape.

faq.q: How often is the SHE Score updated?
faq.a: It is published annually, and quarterly for registered governments. We do not update the published score weekly.

faq.q: Are third-party index scores used to calculate the SHE Score or $SHE?
faq.a: No. Where we show third-party scores (e.g. on a country page), they are for reference only — they are never inputs to the SHE Score or to $SHE token-supply mechanics.

faq.q: What data is the SHE Score built from?
faq.a: Independent institutional data from UN Women, the World Bank, WHO, UNODC, UNESCO, the ILO and the OECD. Each indicator is normalised to 0–100 so higher always means better for women.
